questions.question: A solo song in an opera, oratorio or similar large vocal work. What is this?
questions.hint: The character pauses the story to sing their feelings.
questions.explanation: A solo song in an opera, oratorio or similar large vocal work.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Aria?

answers.text: It is a low double-reed woodwind with a long folded tube.
It is a solo song in an opera, oratorio or similar large vocal work.
It usually has a lyrical, memorable melody with accompaniment.
It usually expresses feelings rather than quickly moving the story forward.

questions.hint: A low double-reed with a long, folded wooden tube.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a low double-reed woodwind with a long folded tube” describes Bassoon, not Aria. A solo song in an opera, oratorio or similar large vocal work.

questions.question: A strophic folk song from North-East Scotland, traditionally sung by farm workers who lived in a bothy. What is this?
questions.hint: North-East farm workers once sang these strophic stories.
questions.explanation: A strophic folk song from North-East Scotland, traditionally sung by farm workers who lived in a bothy.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Bothy ballad?

answers.text: It is a strophic folk song from North-East Scotland.
It is a shallow Irish frame drum played with a double-ended beater.
It is traditionally sung unaccompanied by a male voice, often in Scots dialect.
Its story often concerns farm work, local life or relationships.

questions.hint: An Irish frame drum played with a small beater.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a shallow Irish frame drum played with a double-ended beater” describes Bodhran, not Bothy ballad. A strophic folk song from North-East Scotland, traditionally sung by farm workers who lived in a bothy.

questions.question: A fusion of Celtic or Scottish folk music with rock. What is this?
questions.hint: Fiddle and bagpipes meet electric guitars and drum kit.
questions.explanation: A fusion of Celtic or Scottish folk music with rock.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Celtic rock?

answers.text: It fuses Celtic or Scottish folk music with rock.
It may use traditional instruments and folk melodies.
It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums.
It may add electric guitar, bass guitar, drum kit and amplified vocals.

questions.hint: Two small hand drums joined as a pair.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a joined pair of small, high-pitched hand drums” describes Bongo drums, not Celtic rock. A fusion of Celtic or Scottish folk music with rock.

questions.question: In a large vocal work, this is a group of singers, or music written for them. What is this?
questions.hint: Many singers comment on the action together.
questions.explanation: In a large vocal work, this is a group of singers, or music written for them.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chorus?

answers.text: It is a large group of singers in a work such as an opera or oratorio.
The singers usually perform in several vocal parts.
The group may comment on or take part in the action.
It is a pair of handheld shell-shaped clappers.

questions.hint: Hand-held clappers heard in Spanish dance music.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a pair of handheld shell-shaped clappers” describes Castanets, not Chorus. In a large vocal work, this is a group of singers, or music written for them.

questions.question: Features include clear melody, balanced phrases, mainly homophonic texture and controlled dynamics. What is this?
questions.hint: Balanced phrases, clear textures and restrained expression.
questions.explanation: Features include clear melody, balanced phrases, mainly homophonic texture and controlled dynamics.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Classical?

answers.text: It is a small Scottish Gaelic harp plucked with the fingers.
It belongs to the musical period from about 1750 to 1810.
It often uses clear melodies, balanced phrases and mostly homophonic texture.
Common forms include sonata, concerto, symphony and string quartet.

questions.hint: Scotland's small levered, finger-plucked string instrument.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a small Scottish Gaelic harp plucked with the fingers” describes Clarsach, not Classical. Features include clear melody, balanced phrases, mainly homophonic texture and controlled dynamics.

questions.question: A psalm sung in Scottish Gaelic, traditionally in churches of the Western Isles. What is this?
questions.hint: A precentor leads overlapping, unaccompanied worship singing.
questions.explanation: A psalm sung in Scottish Gaelic, traditionally in churches of the Western Isles.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Gaelic psalm?

answers.text: It is a psalm sung in Scottish Gaelic, traditionally in Western Isles churches.
It tells a player to use a mute.
It is unaccompanied and is often led line by line by a precentor.
The congregation follows in a highly ornamented, overlapping texture.

questions.hint: Fit the device that softens and veils the tone.
questions.explanation: The statement “It tells a player to use a mute” describes Con sordino, not Gaelic psalm. A psalm sung in Scottish Gaelic, traditionally in churches of the Western Isles.

questions.question: Religious vocal music rooted in African American church traditions. What is this?
questions.hint: Church-rooted singing with call and response and hand-clapping.
questions.explanation: Religious vocal music rooted in African American church traditions.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Gospel?

answers.text: It is religious vocal music rooted in African American church traditions.
It often uses expressive singing, call and response and hand-clapping.
A wind or brass player rolls the tongue or uvula while blowing.
It may feature a strong rhythm, improvisation and popular-music instruments.

questions.hint: Roll the tongue while blowing a held note.
questions.explanation: The statement “A wind or brass player rolls the tongue or uvula while blowing” describes Flutter-tonguing, not Gospel. Religious vocal music rooted in African American church traditions.

questions.question: This music often uses sitar, tabla, drone, improvised melody, ornaments and repeating rhythmic cycles. What is this?
questions.hint: Sitar, tabla, drone and repeating rhythmic cycles.
questions.explanation: This music often uses sitar, tabla, drone, improvised melody, ornaments and repeating rhythmic cycles.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Indian?

answers.text: Course examples are often identified by sitar and tabla.
It often features a drone and an improvised, ornamented melody.
It often uses repeating rhythmic cycles.
It is a brass instrument made from a long coil of tubing.

questions.hint: Left hand on valves, right hand inside the bell.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a brass instrument made from a long coil of tubing” describes French horn, not Indian. This music often uses sitar, tabla, drone, improvised melody, ornaments and repeating rhythmic cycles.

questions.question: A style based on short repeated rhythmic or melodic patterns. What is this?
questions.hint: Small repeated patterns change little by little.
questions.explanation: A style based on short repeated rhythmic or melodic patterns.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Minimalist?

answers.text: It is a pair of face-to-face cymbals mounted on a pedal-operated stand.
It is a later-20th-century style based on short repeated patterns.
The music changes gradually by adding, removing or layering notes and parts.
Phasing and additive processes are common techniques.

questions.hint: A foot pedal opens and closes two face-to-face metal discs.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a pair of face-to-face cymbals mounted on a pedal-operated stand” describes Hi-hat cymbals, not Minimalist. A style based on short repeated rhythmic or melodic patterns.

questions.question: An extended form of solo Highland bagpipe music, also called ceol mor. What is this?
questions.hint: Solo Highland bagpipes decorate a theme over steady drones.
questions.explanation: An extended form of solo Highland bagpipe music, also called ceol mor.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pibroch?

answers.text: It is an extended form of solo Highland bagpipe music, also called ceol mor.
It is a medium female voice between soprano and alto.
It develops one theme through increasingly elaborate variations.
It uses many grace notes above continuous drones.

questions.hint: The female voice between soprano and alto.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a medium female voice between soprano and alto” describes Mezzo-soprano, not Pibroch. An extended form of solo Highland bagpipe music, also called ceol mor.

questions.question: A large-scale, multi-movement work for orchestra. What is this?
questions.hint: A large orchestral work, usually in several movements.
questions.explanation: A large-scale, multi-movement work for orchestra.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Symphony?

answers.text: It is a large-scale, multi-movement work for orchestra.
Its movements contrast, and the first is often in sonata form.
Women traditionally sing it while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth.
A Classical example often follows a fast-slow-minuet and trio-fast plan.

questions.hint: Women beat newly woven cloth while singing in Gaelic.
questions.explanation: The statement “Women traditionally sing it while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth” describes Waulking song, not Symphony. A large-scale, multi-movement work for orchestra.

questions.question: A Scottish Gaelic work song sung by women while beating newly woven cloth to soften and shrink it. What is this?
questions.hint: Women beat newly woven cloth while singing in Gaelic.
questions.explanation: A Scottish Gaelic work song sung by women while beating newly woven cloth to soften and shrink it.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Waulking song?

answers.text: Women traditionally sing it while rhythmically beating newly woven cloth.
It is a Scottish Gaelic work song, usually unaccompanied with a steady pulse.
A leader and group often create call and response.
It is a half-sized flute that sounds one octave higher than written.

questions.hint: The half-sized flute that sounds an octave higher.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a half-sized flute that sounds one octave higher than written” describes Piccolo, not Waulking song. A Scottish Gaelic work song sung by women while beating newly woven cloth to soften and shrink it.

questions.question: Music with no established tonal centre and no functional major or minor key. What is this?
questions.hint: No home key.
questions.explanation: Music with no established tonal centre and no functional major or minor key.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Atonal?

answers.text: It uses tonic, subdominant and dominant triads in a major key.
It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.
Its pitches may be treated as equally important.
Its harmony often sounds unsettled or dissonant.

questions.hint: Tonic, subdominant, dominant and submediant power countless major-key songs.
questions.explanation: The statement “It uses tonic, subdominant and dominant triads in a major key” describes Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key, not Atonal. Music with no established tonal centre and no functional major or minor key.

questions.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key
questions.question: A major-key sequence using tonic I, subdominant IV, dominant V and submediant VI. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key

questions.hint: Tonic, subdominant, dominant and submediant power countless major-key songs.
questions.explanation: A major-key sequence using tonic I, subdominant IV, dominant V and submediant VI.

questions.concept: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key
questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key?

answers.text: It uses tonic, subdominant and dominant triads in a major key.
It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.
Chords I, IV and V are major, while chord VI is minor.
These chords create familiar song patterns and the four main cadence types.

questions.hint: No home key.
questions.explanation: The statement “It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key” describes Atonal, not Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key. A major-key sequence using tonic I, subdominant IV, dominant V and submediant VI.

questions.question: Melodic movement or harmony that uses notes a semitone apart, including notes outside the main key. What is this?
questions.hint: All twelve pitches, one piano key at a time.
questions.explanation: Melodic movement or harmony that uses notes a semitone apart, including notes outside the main key.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Chromatic?

answers.text: Its notes move in semitone steps.
It may include notes from outside the main key.
Its six notes are separated only by whole tones, with no semitones.
Its complete scale contains all twelve pitch classes in semitone steps.

questions.hint: Six evenly spaced notes; every step is two semitones.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its six notes are separated only by whole tones, with no semitones” describes Whole-tone scale, not Chromatic. Melodic movement or harmony that uses notes a semitone apart, including notes outside the main key.

questions.question: A dense chord made from adjacent or very closely spaced notes sounded together. What is this?
questions.hint: A fistful of neighbouring notes.
questions.explanation: A dense chord made from adjacent or very closely spaced notes sounded together.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Cluster?

answers.text: It is a dense chord made from adjacent or very closely spaced notes.
It may be played with fingers, a fist, a forearm or several instruments.
It moves from chord V to chord I.

questions.hint: V–I: music's full stop.
questions.explanation: The statement “It moves from chord V to chord I” describes Perfect cadence, not Cluster. A dense chord made from adjacent or very closely spaced notes sounded together.

questions.question: Two melodic parts move in opposite directions at the same time. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key

questions.hint: One part rises while the other falls.
questions.explanation: Two melodic parts move in opposite directions at the same time.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Contrary motion?

answers.text: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.
It involves at least two melodic parts.
The parts move at the same time in opposite directions.
As one part rises, the other falls, or vice versa.

questions.hint: The held note sits above changing harmony, not below.
questions.explanation: The statement “It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below” describes Inverted pedal, not Contrary motion. Two melodic parts move in opposite directions at the same time.

questions.question: A distinct secondary melody heard at the same time as the main melody. What is this?
questions.hint: A second tune sounds alongside and supports the main melody.
questions.explanation: A distinct secondary melody heard at the same time as the main melody.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Countermelody?

answers.text: It is a distinct secondary melody heard with the main melody.
It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.
It has its own recognisable melodic shape.
It complements or contrasts with the main tune while remaining less important.

questions.hint: The held note sits above changing harmony, not below.
questions.explanation: The statement “It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below” describes Inverted pedal, not Countermelody. A distinct secondary melody heard at the same time as the main melody.

questions.question: A high vocal countermelody sung above the main tune. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key

questions.hint: A high vocal tune sits above the main melody, often later.
questions.explanation: A high vocal countermelody sung above the main tune.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Descant (voice)?

answers.text: It is a high vocal countermelody.
It is sung above the main tune.
It is a medium-low adult male voice between tenor and bass.
It often enters in a later verse to add brightness and a fuller texture.

questions.hint: The male voice between tenor and bass.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a medium-low adult male voice between tenor and bass” describes Baritone, not Descant (voice). A high vocal countermelody sung above the main tune.

questions.question: A smooth slide from one pitch to another through the pitches in between. What is this?
questions.hint: Run across piano keys or sweep harp strings between two notes.
questions.explanation: A smooth slide from one pitch to another through the pitches in between.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Glissando?

answers.text: It is a continuous slide from one pitch to another.
It passes rapidly through the pitches in between.
It can be performed by voices, strings, trombone, harp or piano.
It rapidly alternates between a main note and the scale note above.

questions.hint: Two neighbouring notes flutter back and forth.
questions.explanation: The statement “It rapidly alternates between a main note and the scale note above” describes Trill, not Glissando. A smooth slide from one pitch to another through the pitches in between.

questions.question: A very short ornamental note placed immediately before a main note. What is this?
questions.hint: A tiny decorative note just before the main one.
questions.explanation: A very short ornamental note placed immediately before a main note.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Grace note?

answers.text: It is a closing passage added after the main structure.
It is a very short ornamental note immediately before a main note.
It is written smaller than an ordinary note.
It is played quickly so the main note remains the focus.

questions.hint: The musical tail at the very end.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a closing passage added after the main structure” describes Coda, not Grace note. A very short ornamental note placed immediately before a main note.

questions.question: A two-chord cadence ending on V. It sounds open or unfinished because chord I has not arrived. What is this?
questions.hint: The musical comma that ends on chord V.
questions.explanation: A two-chord cadence ending on V. It sounds open or unfinished because chord I has not arrived.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Imperfect cadence?

answers.text: It is a two-chord phrase that ends on chord V.
It moves from chord V to chord I.
The dominant chord creates an expectation of chord I.

questions.hint: V–I: music's full stop.
questions.explanation: The statement “It moves from chord V to chord I” describes Perfect cadence, not Imperfect cadence. A two-chord cadence ending on V. It sounds open or unfinished because chord I has not arrived.

questions.question: A note sustained or repeatedly sounded in an upper part while the harmony beneath it changes. What is this?
questions.hint: The held note sits above changing harmony, not below.
questions.explanation: A note sustained or repeatedly sounded in an upper part while the harmony beneath it changes.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Inverted pedal?

answers.text: It holds or repeats one note in an upper part while harmony changes below.
The sustained note is heard above the changing chords.
It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change.
It is the high-register counterpart of a bass pedal.

questions.hint: The same low melody repeats beneath changing upper parts.
questions.explanation: The statement “It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change” describes Ground bass, not Inverted pedal. A note sustained or repeatedly sounded in an upper part while the harmony beneath it changes.

questions.question: Several notes are sung on a single syllable of text. What is this?

answers.text: Chord progressions - chords I, IV, V and VI in a major key

questions.hint: Many notes share one syllable.
questions.explanation: Several notes are sung on a single syllable of text.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Melismatic?

answers.text: It sings several notes on one syllable.
It contrasts with syllabic word setting.
It gives one note to each syllable.

questions.hint: In ‘music’, sing one note on ‘mu’ and one on ‘sic’.
questions.explanation: The statement “It gives one note to each syllable” describes Syllabic, not Melismatic. Several notes are sung on a single syllable of text.

questions.question: A deliberate change from one key to another within a piece. What is this?
questions.hint: The music moves house to a new key.
questions.explanation: A deliberate change from one key to another within a piece.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Modulation?

answers.text: It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key.
It is a deliberate change from one key to another within a piece.
The new key may be temporary or remain for the rest of the music.
The new key is established through its notes, chords and cadences.

questions.hint: No home key.
questions.explanation: The statement “It has no tonal centre or functional major or minor key” describes Atonal, not Modulation. A deliberate change from one key to another within a piece.

questions.question: A V-I cadence that sounds complete, also called a full close. What is this?
questions.hint: V–I: music's full stop.
questions.explanation: A V-I cadence that sounds complete, also called a full close.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Perfect cadence?

answers.text: It moves from chord V to chord I.
It is a two-chord phrase that ends on chord V.
It gives a strong sense of completion or a full close.

questions.hint: The musical comma that ends on chord V.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a two-chord phrase that ends on chord V” describes Imperfect cadence, not Perfect cadence. A V-I cadence that sounds complete, also called a full close.

questions.question: The pitch of a sounding note is raised or lowered continuously rather than moving in fixed steps. What is this?
questions.hint: A sounding note curves smoothly higher or lower.
questions.explanation: The pitch of a sounding note is raised or lowered continuously rather than moving in fixed steps.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Pitch bend?

answers.text: It raises or lowers the pitch of a sounding note continuously.
The pitch moves smoothly rather than in fixed steps.
Its notes move in semitone steps.
It can use a bent guitar string, pitch wheel, lip pressure or sliding voice.

questions.hint: All twelve pitches, one piano key at a time.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its notes move in semitone steps” describes Chromatic, not Pitch bend. The pitch of a sounding note is raised or lowered continuously rather than moving in fixed steps.

questions.question: The smallest standard interval in Western tonal music, equal to half a tone. What is this?
questions.hint: Move to the very next piano key, black or white.
questions.explanation: The smallest standard interval in Western tonal music, equal to half a tone.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Semitone?

answers.text: It equals half a tone and is the nearest-key distance on a piano.
It is the smallest standard interval in Western tonal music.
On a fretted guitar, it is the distance from one fret to the next.
It equals two semitones, such as C to D.

questions.hint: From C to D: skip the black key between them.
questions.explanation: The statement “It equals two semitones, such as C to D” describes Tone, not Semitone. The smallest standard interval in Western tonal music, equal to half a tone.

questions.question: Each syllable of text is sung to one note. What is this?
questions.hint: In ‘music’, sing one note on ‘mu’ and one on ‘sic’.
questions.explanation: Each syllable of text is sung to one note.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Syllabic?

answers.text: It sings several notes on one syllable.
It gives one note to each syllable.
It helps make sung words clear.
It contrasts with melismatic word setting.

questions.hint: Many notes share one syllable.
questions.explanation: The statement “It sings several notes on one syllable” describes Melismatic, not Syllabic. Each syllable of text is sung to one note.

questions.question: As a pitch interval, it equals two semitones, such as C to D; on a fretted guitar it usually spans two frets. What is this?
questions.hint: From C to D: skip the black key between them.
questions.explanation: As a pitch interval, it equals two semitones, such as C to D; on a fretted guitar it usually spans two frets.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Tone?

answers.text: It equals two semitones, such as C to D.
It equals half a tone and is the nearest-key distance on a piano.
It is twice the size of a semitone.
On a fretted guitar, it usually spans two frets.

questions.hint: Move to the very next piano key, black or white.
questions.explanation: The statement “It equals half a tone and is the nearest-key distance on a piano” describes Semitone, not Tone. As a pitch interval, it equals two semitones, such as C to D; on a fretted guitar it usually spans two frets.

questions.question: A rapid repeated alternation between a main note and the note immediately above it in the scale. What is this?
questions.hint: Two neighbouring notes flutter back and forth.
questions.explanation: A rapid repeated alternation between a main note and the note immediately above it in the scale.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Trill?

answers.text: It rapidly alternates between a main note and the scale note above.
It may be shown by tr., a wavy line or both.
It is a continuous slide from one pitch to another.
It often signals the end of a concerto cadenza.

questions.hint: Run across piano keys or sweep harp strings between two notes.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a continuous slide from one pitch to another” describes Glissando, not Trill. A rapid repeated alternation between a main note and the note immediately above it in the scale.

questions.question: A six-note scale built entirely from whole-tone steps, with no semitones. What is this?
questions.hint: Six evenly spaced notes; every step is two semitones.
questions.explanation: A six-note scale built entirely from whole-tone steps, with no semitones.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Whole-tone scale?

answers.text: Its six notes are separated only by whole tones, with no semitones.
Every step in the scale is the same size.
It weakens the tonic and creates an ambiguous, floating quality.
Its complete scale contains all twelve pitch classes in semitone steps.

questions.hint: All twelve pitches, one piano key at a time.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its complete scale contains all twelve pitch classes in semitone steps” describes Chromatic, not Whole-tone scale. A six-note scale built entirely from whole-tone steps, with no semitones.

questions.question: Metres with two, three or four dotted-crotchet beats per bar. What is this?
questions.hint: Each main beat splits naturally into three.
questions.explanation: Metres with two, three or four dotted-crotchet beats per bar.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Compound time (6/8, 9/8 and 12/8)?

answers.text: It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.
It includes the time signatures 6/8, 9/8 and 12/8.
It has two, three or four dotted-crotchet beats in each bar.
Each main beat divides into three quavers.

questions.hint: Not too fast, not too slow.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow” describes Moderato, not Compound time (6/8, 9/8 and 12/8). Metres with two, three or four dotted-crotchet beats per bar.

questions.question: Different rhythmic patterns sound at the same time, creating competing accents. What is this?
questions.hint: Different rhythmic groupings compete at the same time.
questions.explanation: Different rhythmic patterns sound at the same time, creating competing accents.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Cross rhythms?

answers.text: It places contrasting rhythmic groupings in different parts at the same time.
It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.
The parts imply different accent patterns or competing metres.
Examples include three against two and grouping 4/4 as 3+3+2.

questions.hint: Slow down gradually over a short passage.
questions.explanation: The statement “It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage” describes Ritardando, not Cross rhythms. Different rhythmic patterns sound at the same time, creating competing accents.

questions.question: A moderate tempo marking: neither fast nor slow. What is this?
questions.hint: Not too fast, not too slow.
questions.explanation: A moderate tempo marking: neither fast nor slow.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Moderato?

answers.text: It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.
It is similar to, but not exactly the same as, andante.
It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.
It is often about 108-120 beats per minute, depending on context.

questions.hint: Slow down gradually over a short passage.
questions.explanation: The statement “It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage” describes Ritardando, not Moderato. A moderate tempo marking: neither fast nor slow.

questions.question: The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over a short passage. What is this?
questions.hint: Slow down gradually over a short passage.
questions.explanation: The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over a short passage.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Ritardando?

answers.text: It gradually makes the tempo slower over a short passage.
It has the same practical effect as rallentando.
It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow.

questions.hint: Not too fast, not too slow.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is a moderate tempo marking, neither fast nor slow” describes Moderato, not Ritardando. The tempo gradually becomes slower, usually over a short passage.

questions.question: The performer speeds up and slows down slightly for expression while keeping the music flowing. What is this?
questions.hint: Borrow time, then give it back.
questions.explanation: The performer speeds up and slows down slightly for expression while keeping the music flowing.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Rubato?

answers.text: It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument.
It gives the performer expressive flexibility with tempo.
The performer slightly speeds up and slows down while keeping the broader flow.
It is especially associated with Romantic performance and means 'robbed time'.

questions.hint: The biggest brass voice at the bottom.
questions.explanation: The statement “It is the largest and lowest standard brass instrument” describes Tuba, not Rubato. The performer speeds up and slows down slightly for expression while keeping the music flowing.

questions.question: A repeating accompaniment made from broken chords in the pattern lowest-highest-middle-highest. What is this?
questions.hint: Low–high–middle–high, over and over.
questions.explanation: A repeating accompaniment made from broken chords in the pattern lowest-highest-middle-highest.

questions.question: Which is NOT a feature of Alberti bass?

answers.text: It is a repeating broken-chord accompaniment.
It repeats a bass melody or pattern while upper parts change.
It is often played by the piano left hand in Classical music.

questions.hint: The same low melody repeats beneath changing upper parts.

questions.question: A two-part form with an opening A section followed by a contrasting B section. Either section may repeat. What is this?

questions.hint: The main section keeps returning between contrasting sections.
questions.explanation: The statement “Its main A section returns between contrasting episodes” describes Rondo / ABACA, not Binary / AB. A two-part form with an opening A section followed by a contrasting B section. Either section may repeat.

questions.question: A closing passage added after the main structure of a piece or movement. What is this?

questions.hint: The musical tail at the very end.
